rationale.R1.1: The section asserts a first-ever claim ('first ML method achieving clinically relevant accuracy across diverse target classes') and a clear contribution framing; reviewer must assess whether the thesis and gap statement are well-founded and defensible.
rationale.R1.2: Multiple inferential leaps require checking: (1) from 12% MAE reduction over v1 to 'clinically relevant accuracy'; (2) from three-class evaluation to 'broad applicability'; (3) from consistent cross-target MAE to deployment readiness. Internal coherence of these claims is load-bearing.
rationale.R1.3: The 12% and 28% MAE reductions, and the three per-class MAE values (0.84/0.91/0.79 kcal/mol), are specific quantitative claims that must be traceable to reported experiments, tables, or figures. The comparison baseline ('chemistry-only baseline constructed for this paper') needs verification that it is described and fairly evaluated.
rationale.R1.4: The 'first' claim and the comparison structure (vs. DrugDock-v1, vs. a self-constructed baseline only) raise significant novelty assessment obligations: no comparison to external SOTA methods is visible; the delta vs. nearest prior work is therefore unclear or understated.
rationale.R1.7: Two self-citations are anonymized ('Anonymous Authors et al., 2021/2022'), which is a double-blind compliance issue and also means the provenance of the expanded dataset and the v1 architecture cannot be externally verified. Citation coverage of the broader field (other DTA methods) appears absent.
